metadata.title: SOC Radar Pattern Library for Step 1
metadata.scope: Signal zone taxonomy, disruption filters, classification types, and scoring framework for the Signal Scanner node

sources.reference: Scott D. Anthony, Clayton Christensen, Erik Roth — Seeing What's Next

sources.path: Archive/Signals of Change for BMI Consultants.txt

zones.name: Nonconsumption
zones.description: People who need the outcome but cannot access, afford, or use today's solutions

zones.name: Overserved Customers
zones.description: Customers paying for performance they do not use or value
zones.watch_for: Feature fatigue, willingness-to-pay erosion, demand for simpler alternatives

zones.name: Low-End Foothold
zones.description: Simpler, cheaper alternatives gaining traction with less-demanding segments
zones.watch_for: Good-enough solutions at lower price points entering from below

zones.name: New-Market Foothold
zones.description: Solutions creating demand where none existed before
zones.watch_for: New categories, new use cases, previously non-consuming populations adopting

zones.name: Business Model Anomaly
zones.description: Entrants using fundamentally different cost structures, channels, or revenue models
zones.watch_for: SaaS vs perpetual license, marketplace vs direct, free-tier vs enterprise

zones.name: Enabling Technology
zones.description: Technologies that reduce barriers to entry or change the cost curve
zones.watch_for: Open-source releases, patent expirations, capability milestones, cost inflection points

zones.name: Regulatory / Policy Shift
zones.description: Rule changes that open or close access to markets
zones.watch_for: New compliance requirements, deregulation, standards changes, trade policy shifts

disruption_filters.threshold_note: A signal that passes 3+ filters warrants deeper investigation. 4+ filters indicates high disruptive potential.

filters.name: Asymmetric Motivation
filters.question: Would an incumbent choose not to respond because the opportunity looks unattractive at their scale?

filters.name: Asymmetric Skills
filters.question: Does the entrant possess capabilities the incumbent lacks or cannot easily replicate?

filters.name: Trajectory
filters.question: Is the entrant improving fast enough to eventually meet mainstream needs?

filters.name: Performance Overshoot
filters.question: Has the incumbent's product performance exceeded what mainstream customers actually need?

filters.name: Barrier Removal
filters.question: Does the entrant eliminate a skill, wealth, or access barrier that kept people from consuming?

filters.name: Business Model Conflict
filters.question: Would responding require the incumbent to cannibalize margins, channels, or key relationships?

classification_types.name: Sustaining
classification_types.description: Improvements along dimensions incumbents already compete on. Incumbents are motivated and able to respond.

classification_types.name: Disruptive — Low-End
classification_types.description: Simpler, cheaper entrant targeting the least-profitable customers first, then moving upmarket
classification_types.strategic_response: Create separate unit, partner, or change business model

classification_types.name: Disruptive — New-Market
classification_types.description: Entrant serving nonconsumers or creating a new context for consumption
classification_types.strategic_response: Create separate unit, partner, or change business model

levels.1: Affects a niche we do not serve
levels.2: Affects an adjacent segment or channel
levels.3: Threatens core revenue or key customer relationships

levels.1: Improving slowly; years away from mainstream
levels.2: Improving steadily; could reach mainstream in 2-4 years
levels.3: Improving rapidly; viable for mainstream within 1-2 years

action_tiers.action: Design and run an experiment within 60 days

rpv_framework.name: Resources, Processes, Values
rpv_framework.purpose: Assess whether incumbent can structurally respond to a disruptive signal

dimensions.name: Resources
dimensions.question: Can the incumbent deploy the capital, talent, and technology needed?

dimensions.name: Processes
dimensions.question: Are the incumbent's ways of working optimized for this response, or would they need to operate differently?

dimensions.name: Values
dimensions.question: Does the incumbent's cost structure and margin expectations make the response attractive?

rpv_framework.interpretation: If all three align, the incumbent can and probably will respond. If Values or Processes are misaligned, the incumbent faces a structural barrier even with sufficient resources.

confidence_ratings.guidance: Treat as reliable; use in client presentations

confidence_ratings.meaning: Some evidence exists but gaps remain
confidence_ratings.guidance: Usable for prioritization; flag the gap in deliverable

confidence_ratings.meaning: Assessment is based on inference, not observation
confidence_ratings.guidance: Do not present as finding — treat as hypothesis to test

step_1_behavior: Use the 7 signal zones as the classification taxonomy. Every detected signal must map to exactly one zone.
Apply all 6 disruption filters to each signal classified as potentially disruptive.
Classify signals as Sustaining, Disruptive — Low-End, or Disruptive — New-Market.
Score using Impact × Speed formula. Output the tier assignment.
Capture all 6 required evidence fields for each signal.
Flag signal zones with no coverage as intelligence blind spots.
Do not fabricate signal zone names or filter names — use only the set defined in this file.
